name: From Facture-Biganos to Bordeaux
description:
The smell you will smell when you get off the train: it is the stationery of Facture-Biganos, depending on the direction of the wind, you can even smell this smell as far as Bordeaux on certain days.

Paradoxically, you will go in the opposite direction to which Bordeaux is located. This allows you to avoid a very busy axis and to engage in singles that will put your arms to the test until Pierroton.

Once out of this immense straight line, you can let go and have fun in a long sequence of singles at Cestas level, these parts are not too technical, they are playful to allow you to apprehend good sensations in gravel.

A section then continues along the Eau Bourde which runs into the Garonne.

Crossing the Parc du Moulin d’Ornon and the Priory of Cayac, you enter a walking area, so be respectful of the walkers who find themselves there.

This part is a real green thread that leads to the gates of Bordeaux.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Biganos offers access to a diverse landscape, characterized by the Leyre Delta, often called the "Little Amazon," with its lush, tunnel-like forest canopy and wetland trails. The region is also bordered by the vast Landes de Gascogne forest, providing extensive shaded routes through maritime pines. Proximity to Arcachon Bay ensures a mix of coastal paths, marshlands, and greenways, creating varied terrain for gravel enthusiasts. This blend of natural features provides a wide array of surfaces suitable for different gravel biking experiences. Best gravel bike trails around Biganos The most popular gravel bike trail is From Facture-Biganos to Bordeaux, a 33.3 miles (53.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 20 minutes to complete. This route connects Biganos to Bordeaux, traversing varied terrain. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Port of La Test de Buch – The basin at Gujan Mestras loop from Le petit train, a difficult 32.1 miles (51.6 km) path. This extensive loop explores the Arcachon Bay area, offering a mix of coastal and hinterland views. Local gravel bikers also love the Le Teich cycle path – Cycle path in La Teste loop from Le Teich, a 19.0 miles (30.6 km) trail leading through the Arcachon Bay area, often completed in about 1 hour 47 minutes. Gravel biking around Biganos is defined by the Leyre Delta's wetlands, the extensive Landes de Gascogne forest, and the coastal paths of Arcachon Bay. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Biganos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 40 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Biganos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available around Biganos?
A: There are over 15 gravel bike trails around Biganos, offering a diverse range of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails in Biganos?
A: Gravel biking in Biganos offers a varied terrain, including paved cycle paths, forest trails through the vast Landes de Gascogne forest, marshlands within the Leyre Delta, greenways, and small roads. This mix provides options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What makes Biganos a unique destination for gravel biking?
A: Biganos is unique due to its location at the mouth of the Leyre river, often called the 'Little Amazon,' with lush, tunnel-like forest canopies and wetland trails. It's also bordered by the extensive Landes de Gascogne forest and offers proximity to the Arcachon Bay, providing a rich blend of natural environments for gravel enthusiasts.

Q: What is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Biganos?
A: The region around Biganos is generally pleasant for gravel biking outside of the peak summer heat. Spring and autumn offer mild temperatures and beautiful scenery. While the varied terrain can handle different conditions, checking local weather forecasts is always recommended to avoid overly muddy or dusty trails.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or natural features to see along the gravel routes in Biganos?
A: Yes, many routes pass by notable features. You can explore the traditional &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3968440" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port and beach of Le Teich&lt;/a&gt;, or cycle near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1258165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Teich Ornithological Reserve&lt;/a&gt;. The Leyre Delta itself is a significant natural feature, and some trails offer views of the Arcachon Bay, such as from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3550270" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Bay&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I explore the Leyre Delta by bike?
A: Absolutely. The Leyre Delta, often referred to as the 'Little Amazon,' features unique gravel paths and trails that wind through wetlands and flood-prone woodlands. These paths offer an immersive experience in a preserved natural setting, allowing you to discover its rich biodiversity. For more information on the delta, you can visit &lt;a href="https://tourisme-coeurdubassin.com/en/the-heart-of-the-bay/le-delta-de-la-leyre/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;tourisme-coeurdubassin.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any coastal views or beach access points on Biganos gravel routes?
A: While Biganos is slightly inland, its proximity to Arcachon Bay means many routes connect to coastal paths and offer views of the bay. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41569730" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pereire Beach, Arcachon – The basin at Gujan Mestras loop from Le Teich&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the Arcachon Bay area, and you can reach spots like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/446944" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Eyrac Beach, Arcachon&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5782521" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bassin d'Arcachon Beach and Marina&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find parking for gravel biking in Biganos?
A: Many of the traditional ports in Biganos, such as Port de Biganos and Port des Tuiles, serve as charming starting points and often have parking facilities. Additionally, trailheads for routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1068282239/from-facture-biganos-to-bordeaux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Facture-Biganos to Bordeaux&lt;/a&gt; will have designated parking areas, typically near the starting points in Facture-Biganos.

Q: Is public transport available to access gravel trails in Biganos?
A: Biganos is connected to the regional transport network, making it possible to access some trailheads via public transport. Specific bus routes or train stations might serve areas close to popular cycling paths. It's advisable to check local public transport schedules and bike carriage policies before planning your trip.

Q: Are there family-friendly gravel biking options in Biganos?
A: Yes, Biganos offers several family-friendly gravel biking options. The region's mix of paved cycle paths and less challenging forest trails provides routes suitable for various ages and skill levels. Look for routes with lower elevation gains and shorter distances for a comfortable family outing.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes around Biganos?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37605633" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The basin at Gujan Mestras – Pointe du Cap Ferret loop from Hougueyra&lt;/a&gt; offers an extensive loop exploring the Arcachon Bay area. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39295726" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Le Teich cycle path – Cycle path in La Teste loop from Le Teich&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 30.6 km ride.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the gravel trails in Biganos?
A: Many natural areas and trails in Biganos are dog-friendly, especially those through the Landes de Gascogne forest and along the Leyre Delta. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash and check for any specific local regulations or protected areas, such as the Teich Ornithological Reserve, where rules might be stricter.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay near the gravel trails in Biganos?
A: Biganos and the surrounding towns offer various cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options that cater to visitors. The charming ports of Biganos are good starting points with amenities nearby. For specific recommendations, local tourist information centers can provide details on cyclist-friendly establishments.

Q: What is the difficulty level of gravel biking routes in Biganos?
A: The gravel biking routes around Biganos cater to a range of abilities. You can find easy rides, such as those on dedicated cycle paths, as well as moderate and difficult excursions with more varied terrain and elevation changes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28893332" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pereire Beach, Arcachon – Seaside Cycle Path loop from Gujan-Mestras&lt;/a&gt; is rated moderate, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37605633" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The basin at Gujan Mestras – Pointe du Cap Ferret loop from Hougueyra&lt;/a&gt; is considered difficult.
tips: This section of path takes you to the heart of a landscape typical of the pine forests of southern Gironde. There are sandy portions that will make you appreciate the return to the bitumen.
These playful tracks will allow you to take full advantage of a hybrid, gravel or even mountain bike outing. It smells of pines, it smells of summer!

This pretty wooded and landscaped park is home to a priory dating from the 12th century. It is a pleasant place for a walk at the gates of the city. Following the Eau Bourde river, you reach the Ornon mill garden and, further still, the Anduche wood park.

waypoints.name: Cayac Priory and the "La Reine de la Rive" statue
